evidence: goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go defines a large composite Runtime (RuntimeContainerOps, RuntimeUrbitOps, RuntimeSnapshotOps, RuntimeConfigOps, RuntimeLoadOps, RuntimeServiceOps) plus multiple narrow runtime variants, each composed of many function fields.

goseg/startup_orchestrator.go and goseg/main.go define additional large callback bags (startupSubsystemInitializationRuntime, startBackgroundServicesRuntime and sub-structs), creating another seam layer with dozens of callbacks.

goseg/docker/network/network.go and goseg/docker/orchestration/wireguard.go repeatedly expose public function -> default runtime constructor -> withRuntime function chains that mostly forward calls with little domain policy added.

goseg/config/config_view.go exposes a broad ConfigView interface (many settings families) and many global snapshot wrappers; consumers like goseg/importer/importer.go and goseg/routines/healthcheck/disk.go only need small slices, but still depend on globally materialized config snapshots.

Multiple modules rely on mutable package-level seams instead of explicit dependency objects (`goseg/system/wifi.go`, `goseg/logger/logger.go`, `goseg/config/version.go`), and tests repeatedly restore globals (`goseg/system/wifi_test.go`, `goseg/logger/logger_test.go`, `goseg/importer/importer_test.go`).

Session connection management combines registry mutation, auth/unauth transitions, broadcast behavior, and stale cleanup in one type (`goseg/session/ws.go`) while sharing additional global state entrypoints in `goseg/session/session.go`.
Transition workflows are heavily duplicated via repeated template calls and mirrored transition-key handling (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/rectify/rectify.go`).

Docker runtime seams and startup/runtime callback structs are excessively wide, creating callback-bag coupling (`goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go`, `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go`, `goseg/startup_orchestrator_test.go`).

System package files combine unrelated concerns (metrics, filesystem provisioning, crontab mutation, WiFi shelling) in shared modules (`goseg/system/system.go`, `goseg/system/disk.go`, `goseg/system/wifi.go`).

`goseg/routines/version.go` has public wrappers (`StartVersionSubsystem`, `CheckVersionLoop`) that explicitly discard errors from their `WithContext` variants via `_ = ...`, while sibling orchestration code generally returns/propagates errors.
`goseg/main.go` `startDevMode` launches `http.ListenAndServe` in a goroutine without handling its returned error, creating another fire-and-forget failure path.
`goseg/docker/network/network.go` `killContainerUsingPortWithRuntime` returns raw `DockerClientNewFn` errors (`return err`) while most neighboring functions wrap with operation context.
`goseg/system/disk.go` `CreateGroundSegFilesystem` forwards several nested errors without adding step context (`return "", err`), unlike other functions in the same file that wrap failures.

`goseg/rectify/rectify.go` and `goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/docker.go` consume channels without closure checks (`event := <-ch`, `event := <-messages`, `err := <-errs`), so closed channels can degrade into silent zero-value handling/looping instead of explicit terminal errors.

findings.summary: Runtime seam structs have grown into callback registries that increase wiring and test indirection more than they reduce coupling.
evidence: runtime_seams.go defines multiple layered runtime structs with many function members and option-merging paths, while startup_orchestrator.go introduces another independent callback-runtime hierarchy for startup/background services.
runtime_seams_test.go and startup_orchestrator_test.go spend substantial code constructing stub callback bags and nil-guard wrappers, indicating the seam shape itself is a major test burden.
These seams frequently expose low-level operations directly (container, urbit, config, service ops) rather than cohesive domain-oriented ports, so call sites must understand broad internal mechanics.
findings.suggestion: Collapse callback bags into a smaller set of cohesive domain ports (for example TransitionOps, HealthOps, StartupOps) with focused method sets; move default implementations behind constructor functions and prefer typed interfaces over per-function fields to reduce assembly overhead.

findings.summary: Many exported APIs are thin pass-through wrappers that add call depth without adding policy or translation.
evidence: network.go repeats a pattern like ExportedFn -> XWithRuntime(defaultRuntime, args) -> withDefaults -> concrete logic for nearly every operation (KillContainerUsingPort, GetContainerNetwork, CreateVolume, DeleteVolume, WriteFileToVolume, VolumeExists, AddOrGetNetwork).

wireguard.go duplicates the same structure (LoadWireguard/loadWireguard, wgContainerConf/wgContainerConfForRuntime, buildWgConf/buildWgConfForRuntime, WriteWgConf/writeWgConf, WriteWgConfToFile/writeWgConfToFileForRuntime, copyWGFileToVolume/copyWGFileToVolumeForRuntime).
rectify.go adds additional defaulting wrappers (NewRectifyRuntime + withDefaultsRectifyRuntime + context/non-context wrappers), expanding API surface even when behavior is mostly the same.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on a single internal runtime entrypoint per domain and keep only one external API per behavior; use runtime injection at constructor/module level instead of per-function wrapper pairs.

findings.summary: Config snapshot abstractions act as a broad service locator, weakening boundary clarity between subsystems.
evidence: ConfigView in config_view.go aggregates many unrelated concerns (auth, update, swap, runtime, connectivity, startram, etc.) into one interface, then global snapshot helper functions expose each slice again.
importer.go depends on config.NewConfigViewSnapshot() and reads SwapSettings from a full snapshot object during initialization, tying importer behavior to global config materialization.
healthcheck/disk.go reads HealthCheckSettingsSnapshot and writes back via global UpdateConfTyped function variables, combining read/write global config dependencies in a single routine.
config/config.go still maintains mutable process-level runtime context and startram config globals, so snapshot abstractions do not fully isolate callers from shared mutable state.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad ConfigView usage with narrow, feature-specific settings providers (for example SwapConfigProvider, HealthCheckConfigProvider) injected per subsystem; keep mutable global config behind explicit domain services and reduce package-level snapshot helpers.

findings.summary: Parallel context and non-context function surfaces duplicate behavior and widen abstraction surface unnecessarily.
evidence: system.go duplicates routine entrypoints (StartBackupRoutines/StartBackupRoutinesWithContext, StartChopRoutines/StartChopRoutinesWithContext, plus helper variants) with mostly shared logic.
healthcheck/disk.go duplicates SmartDiskCheck and DiskUsageWarning with corresponding WithContext forms that largely replicate loop bodies and differ only in cancellation handling.
logs.go repeats OldLogsCleaner/SysLogStreamer/DockerLogStreamer and corresponding WithContext variants, adding API and maintenance overhead.
rectify.go similarly keeps both non-context and context variants for transition handlers.
findings.suggestion: Make context-aware functions the single canonical implementation and keep non-context entrypoints as minimal one-line adapters (or remove them where not required externally), centralizing loop logic to one path.

findings.summary: Global mutable seam variables create hidden coupling and force broad state-reset scaffolding across modules.
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi.go` stores behavior behind package vars (`execCommandForWiFi`, `runCommandForWiFi`, `wifiNewClientForWiFi`, etc.), then tests must restore each seam in `resetWifiSeamsForTest`.

`goseg/logger/logger.go` keeps mutable globals (`sysLogSink`, `mkdirAllFn`, `pathResolverFn`, init state), and tests use dedicated reset helpers (`resetLoggerGlobalsForTest`, `resetLoggerInitForTest`).
`goseg/config/version.go` keeps mutable process-wide fetch store/client/retry vars (`versionStore`, `versionHTTPClient`, retry settings), coupling call sites to shared state.
findings.suggestion: Introduce explicit dependency structs instantiated per subsystem (or per test) and thread them through constructors/start functions; keep package globals as immutable defaults only.

findings.summary: Session connection code conflates lifecycle, auth-bucket transitions, broadcast delivery, and placeholder session behavior in one component.
evidence: `transitionClientStateLocked` in `goseg/session/ws.go` handles state transition logic, active-flag semantics, and cross-bucket mutation in one path.
`enqueueFakeConnIfNilOrUnconnected` inserts empty `MuConn` placeholders, creating a hidden pseudo-state rather than explicit modeling.
`session.go` also exposes global access/mutation APIs for manager and log session state, widening the surface coupled to this manager behavior.
findings.suggestion: Split responsibilities into separate collaborators: connection registry, auth-state transition service, and broadcast dispatcher; replace fake placeholder connections with an explicit pending/auth token state model.

findings.summary: Urbit transition operations are implemented as repeated near-identical templates with hardcoded status/event strings.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` repeats `runUrbitTransitionTemplateFn` blocks across many operations (`setUrbitDomain`, `setMinIODomain`, `toggleChopOnVereUpdate`, `togglePower`, `toggleDevMode`, `toggleNetwork`) with similar lifecycle metadata and different inner actions.
`goseg/rectify/rectify.go` maintains large transition reducer maps with one-field assignments per transition key, mirroring the same transition taxonomy in a separate mechanism.
findings.suggestion: Define a declarative transition specification table (transition type, lifecycle events, action function, post-hooks) and drive both operation dispatch and reducer wiring from shared metadata.

findings.summary: Runtime seams use oversized callback bags that obscure subsystem boundaries and increase wiring complexity.
evidence: `goseg/docker/lifecycle/lifecycle.go` `Runtime` has many behavior fields and matching `With...` setters, making dependency shape broad and mutable.

`goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` `Runtime` embeds multiple large ops structs; transition-specific subsets are manually carved out (`NewDockerTransitionRuntime`, `NewDockerHealthRuntime`).
`goseg/startup_orchestrator_test.go` constructs very large runtime callback structs to stand up tests, indicating high orchestration coupling overhead.
findings.suggestion: Replace broad callback bags with narrower interfaces per workflow (startup bootstrap, transition handling, health checks) and provide composable adapter constructors that hide unrelated callbacks from each caller.

findings.summary: System modules blend unrelated host concerns, reducing cohesion and making change/testing boundaries unclear.
evidence: `goseg/system/system.go` combines telemetry gathering, disk label parsing, fixer script generation, and crontab mutation.
`goseg/system/disk.go` includes SMART checks, fstab reconciliation, filesystem creation/mount orchestration, and temp-dir cleanup policies.
`goseg/system/wifi.go` combines WiFi loop lifecycle, shell-command orchestration, captive rules, and connection state globals.
findings.suggestion: Refactor `system` into focused services (host metrics, storage provisioning, wifi networking, maintenance jobs) with clear public interfaces and package-level ownership boundaries.

findings.summary: Non-context entrypoints suppress background subsystem failures instead of surfacing them consistently.
evidence: `StartVersionSubsystem()` and `CheckVersionLoop()` use `_ = ...WithContext(...)`, dropping terminal errors from version/update loops.
`startDevMode()` runs `go http.ListenAndServe("0.0.0.0:6060", nil)` and ignores returned listener errors entirely.
findings.suggestion: Standardize launcher behavior: either return errors to callers or report through a shared error channel/logger callback with structured context; avoid `_ =` on fallible subsystem startups.

findings.summary: Some boundary functions return raw nested errors, making failure provenance inconsistent across modules.
evidence: `killContainerUsingPortWithRuntime` returns raw client-construction error (`return err`) while other network functions wrap with operation context.
`CreateGroundSegFilesystem` returns raw errors from `nextGroundSegPath`, `formatGroundSegFilesystem`, and `reconcileGroundSegFstab` without annotating step/context.
`syncRetrieveWithStartram` returns upstream error directly (`return err`) despite surrounding startup code typically wrapping failures with subsystem labels.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a consistent boundary rule: wrap propagated errors at module/service boundaries with operation-specific context using `%w`, and reserve raw propagation for tightly scoped helper internals only.

findings.summary: Multiple long-running loops read channels without closure checks, so producer shutdown is not handled as an explicit failure mode.
evidence: `runTransitionEventLoop` uses `case event := <-ch` with no `ok` check; closed channels can repeatedly deliver zero values and bypass explicit termination/error reporting.

`dockerListenerWithContext` reads `event := <-messages` and `err := <-errs` without `ok`; when channels close, logic falls into nil/zero-value paths (`continue` on nil err) instead of deterministic shutdown error semantics.
findings.suggestion: Use two-value receives (`v, ok := <-ch`) in long-lived loops; treat `!ok` as a terminal boundary and either return a wrapped error or exit explicitly with a clear reason.
